critique.summary: The article describes a Chrome extension called Speech Speed that dynamically adjusts video playback speed based on the speaker's syllable rate. It uses audio processing techniques to detect the natural speaking rate and modifies playback speed to normalize speech to a comfortable listening rate.
mainPoints: The extension captures audio from the largest video element on a page using captureStream() without interrupting playback.
It isolates the vowel formant frequency range (300-3000 Hz) to focus on syllable energy and uses an energy-envelope modulation analysis to detect syllable rate.
The detected syllable rate is converted into a playback speed that speeds up slow speakers more and fast speakers less, aiming for a target syllable rate (default 9 syl/s).
Speed adjustments are smoothed to avoid jarring changes and revert to normal speed during silence.
The extension includes a diagnostic overlay for real-time feedback and offers configurable parameters for tuning detection and speed control.
Known limitations include incompatibility with DRM-protected content, potential false detections with music or sound effects, and challenges with multiple speakers or non-speech audio.
critique.truthfulness: The technical details provided about audio capture, filtering, syllable detection, and speed adjustment align with established audio processing principles and web APIs such as captureStream() and Web Audio API. The description of challenges with DRM content and ambient noise is consistent with known browser and media restrictions. The repository is hosted on GitHub with code and documentation supporting these claims, indicating the article is accurate and truthful in its explanation of the extension's functionality and limitations.
considerations: The extension does not work on DRM-protected streaming services like Netflix or Disney+ due to browser security restrictions.
Accuracy may degrade in videos with heavy background music, sound effects, or multiple overlapping speakers.
The extension requires user installation and enabling developer mode in Chrome, which may limit accessibility for some users.
Performance impact on CPU usage due to frequent audio processing is not discussed.
User experience may vary depending on the chosen target syllable rate and speed limits.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion revolves around a Chrome extension that adjusts video playback speed based on speaker pace. Users share mixed experiences, with some appreciating the concept and others skeptical about its effectiveness or wary of security risks related to browser extensions.

comments.author: diacritical
comments.text:
For most videos the speaker talks in a relatively constant speed throughout the video. I've been using Video Speed Controller on Firefox for years. Sadly, it's not actively monitored by Mozilla for security issues even though it has 100k+ downloads. It basically lets you change the speed by whatever increments you want with a key. If the speaker slows down, tap-tap-tap, you can speed it up. I don't really see the need for algorithms or AI to solve what's already almost-solved.

I don't get why this addon isn't included in Firefox to begin with. Maybe because it doesn't just work only on standard HTML5 video files, but has support for specific sites and their players, too? But I've seen several updates on Firefox (not about the addon) that are along the lines of "solved perf issues on $site". So it's not like Firefox is trying to be site-agnostic.
comments.analysis: This comment provides a well-informed perspective on existing solutions and raises valid points about the necessity of AI for this problem.

comments.author: Bolwin
comments.text: Very interesting idea, but doesn't seem to work very well. I tried on three videos. On two it miscounted to an absurdly low rate of ~1 syllable/s and played it at the max speed which was illegible and in one it failed to detect any speech at all.
comments.analysis: The user shares firsthand experience highlighting accuracy issues with the extension, which is a relevant critique of its current performance.

comments.author: hackemmy
comments.text: This is really cool. I build Chrome extensions too and the hardest part with anything audio related is getting the detection right across different types of content. Lectures vs podcasts vs casual vlogs all have very different speech patterns. I wonder if this could also work with non-English content since syllable detection probably breaks down with tonal languages.
comments.analysis: This comment insightfully discusses technical challenges in speech detection across diverse content and languages, showing domain knowledge.

comments.author: ghm2199
comments.text:
I would never install an extension with a 1000 foot pole, because of its seriously flawed security model. I think in 2026 if you really badly need an extension as an end user, write your own or pay someone to do it. I would also accept open source ones, since I can review the code.

Your extension can and will likely read all your data and likely has broad permissions to just fax all your Gmail to remote cloud server.
comments.analysis: Raises valid security concerns about browser extensions and offers practical advice for cautious users.

comments.author: golfer
comments.text:
This is a very clever idea. Unfortunately I dislike installing extensions, because so many of them seem to get taken over by nefarious owners. Sadly it's poisoned the whole extension platform for me.

FYI: On Youtube, the keyboard shortcut for changing the video speed is simply pressing < or >
comments.analysis: Expresses a common user concern about extension security and provides useful alternative information.

comments.author: ortusdux
comments.text: This would be a killer feature for a podcast app.  I can't stomach NPR content unless it's sped up to almost 2x, with the exception of a few fast talking hosts.
comments.analysis: Offers a practical use case for the extension, reflecting real user preferences for audio content speed.

comments.author: apparent
comments.text: Pretty cool, would be great if it could detect accented speech and have a different normalized speed for that. I listen to most people at 2x or more, but I can't usually understand accented English quite that fast.
comments.analysis: Highlights a nuanced limitation related to speech variation, suggesting improvements for broader usability.

comments.author: jauntywundrkind
comments.text: How good is the web's support for having subtitles? I'd love a version of this that just worked off of subtitles, rather than audio processing.
comments.analysis: Proposes a potentially simpler and more reliable alternative approach, showing constructive thinking.

comments.author: Mc_Big_G
comments.text: I have a policy against installing extensions but I might make an exception for this one.  I default to 2x and often have to go to 3x if the speaker is really slow.  The one exception is music production related videos and it is sometimes excruciating to get through.  This is most likely because I'm usually watching some kind of instructional or educational video and just want the content and have no interest in being "entertained". That said, I understand that you have to game YouTube's algo and rules in order to make even a tiny bit of money. Imagine if every channel cut the first 30 seconds of every video intro and everyone could just get to the point.
comments.analysis: Provides personal context on video speed preferences and insight into content creator behavior.

comments.author: LowLevelKernel
comments.text: Wow. I’ve been looking for this from a long time.
comments.analysis: Expresses enthusiasm but lacks substantive detail or critique.

comments.author: shevy-java
comments.text:
Damn it!

Now I am called The Snail Speaker. :(

What I found interesting is ... if you have some recorded lectures from a university, some speakers are ultra-fast at 1x speed; and some others are acceptable once at 1.5 or 1.75.

Most I find difficult to understand at 2.0x though, so I mostly settled between the range of 1.25 to 1.5, sometimes 1.75 (depends on the topic too, of course; some topic is harder to understand, but here my thought was that some are able to speak fast and others can not. It's almost a skill to have or not have).
comments.analysis: Shares nuanced personal experience about speech speed comprehension, adding depth to the discussion.
